# From Single Prompts to Retrieval-Augmented Generation

The practical deployment of large language models (LLMs) in clinical research begins with the most elementary interaction: a natural-language prompt submitted to a general-purpose model. Despite its apparent simplicity, this interface has proved surprisingly capable, and a growing body of evidence maps both its reach and its limits across a wide spectrum of clinical tasks. More sophisticated strategies—structured prompt engineering, retrieval augmentation, and domain-specific fine-tuning—have been developed to push performance toward clinical utility, each adding a layer of architectural complexity in exchange for measurable gains in accuracy and safety.

## Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Prompting in Clinical Contexts

In zero-shot settings, an LLM is asked to perform a clinical task without any task-specific examples embedded in the prompt. @Naliyatthaliyazchayil2025Evaluating evaluated five models—ChatGPT-4, LLaMA-3.1, Gemini-1.5, DeepSeek-R1, and OpenAI-O3—on three zero-shot tasks: primary diagnosis generation from structured patient data, ICD-10-CM code assignment, and 30-day readmission risk stratification. The reasoning-optimized models (DeepSeek-R1 and OpenAI-O3) outperformed their non-reasoning counterparts on diagnostic accuracy, but all models exhibited pronounced variability on medical coding tasks, where the combinatorial complexity of ICD-10-CM's more than 70,000 codes challenges even carefully tuned retrieval pipelines. The discrepancy between knowledge-level benchmarks and practice-level performance is a recurring theme in the literature: @Gong2025KnowledgePractice, in a systematic review of 39 medical LLM benchmarks, documented that while models routinely exceed human performance on licensing examinations, their translation to real clinical tasks remains poorly characterized across specialties.

Subspecialty evaluations have probed this gap further. @Beil2025Comparative compared Claude and ChatGPT-4 on cardiothoracic anesthesia board-style questions, finding that subspecialty question sets expose weaknesses not apparent on general medical examinations. Similarly, @Yorgancolu2025Performance conducted a prospective cross-sectional study pitting ChatGPT and Gemini against emergency physicians on NSTEMI case vignettes, revealing that LLMs were competitive on guideline-concordant management steps but struggled with nuanced clinical judgment under time pressure. @Polus2026Adherence assessed adherence of three free-tier models—ChatGPT-5.2, Gemini 3 Flash, and Claude—to the 2024 European Society of Cardiology hypertension guidelines, demonstrating variability in guideline fidelity that has direct implications for clinical decision support.

A meta-analysis by @Zhang2025Leveraging, encompassing studies of dental patient education published between November 2022 and June 2024, synthesized accuracy estimates across LLM-answered patient inquiries using a modified QUADAS-2 framework, finding moderate accuracy with substantial heterogeneity across question domains—a pattern consistent with the broader observation that zero-shot LLMs perform well on factual recall but less reliably on tasks requiring nuanced context integration. Together, these findings establish that zero-shot prompting provides a serviceable baseline but is insufficient for high-stakes clinical applications without deliberate augmentation.

## Prompt Engineering for Clinical Research

Systematic manipulation of the prompt structure—rather than changes to model weights—can markedly improve LLM performance on clinical tasks. Several strategies have emerged as particularly effective in medical contexts.

Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting, which instructs the model to generate explicit intermediate reasoning steps before committing to an answer, is especially well matched to clinical reasoning, where the logical chain from symptom to diagnosis must be transparent and defensible. @Shah2026Integrating tested three distinct prompt templates on de-identified radiology and pathology notes from 20 breast cancer patients, asking models to extract variables required for two established sentinel lymph node metastasis nomograms. The o1 model with chain-of-thought structuring achieved the most accurate extraction, demonstrating that eliciting step-by-step reasoning materially improves precision in information extraction tasks that involve multiple conditional variables.

Role-based prompting—assigning the model an expert clinical persona before querying it—represents another widely applied technique. By prefacing queries with instructions such as "You are an experienced oncologist reviewing a staging report," researchers have observed improvements in response specificity and reduction in generic hedging. @Kim2025Using used structured prompts with explicit role assignment to direct GPT-4 in extracting colorectal cancer clinical stages from imaging reports according to AJCC TNM criteria, reporting acceptable concordance with expert-annotated gold standards in a pilot series, while noting that ambiguous radiological language remained a source of disagreement.

Iterative prompt refinement—where an initial prompt is systematically revised based on error analysis—has been applied to administrative tasks as well as clinical reasoning. @Wang2026Validation developed a GPT-4o–based ICD-10-CM validation system through iterative prompt engineering applied to the MIMIC-IV database, ultimately evaluating 13,102 codes. The resulting system achieved performance superior to rule-based alternatives on prevalent diagnoses, with a structured template that decomposed the validation task into discrete sub-steps. Such decomposition mirrors human expert workflows and has been identified as a key principle for reliable LLM deployment in clinical informatics.

The MedHELM evaluation framework introduced by @Bedi2026Holistic illustrates the consequences of prompt strategy at scale: across a clinician-validated taxonomy of five clinical task categories—including clinical decision support, note generation, and patient communication—performance on practice-based tasks lagged substantially behind that on knowledge-based benchmarks, underscoring that prompt structure must be tailored to the operational context rather than assumed to generalize from examination settings.

## Retrieval-Augmented Generation

The principal failure mode of general-purpose LLMs in medicine is hallucination: the confident generation of plausible but factually incorrect clinical statements. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) addresses this by coupling the LLM to an external, curated knowledge base, retrieving relevant documents at inference time and conditioning the model's response on verified content. This architecture converts an LLM from a closed parametric system into an open retrieval-reader pipeline, enabling factual grounding without retraining.

@Fukui2025Evaluation developed a RAG system grounded in 40 institution-specific nuclear medicine examination manuals from a single Japanese hospital. Because nuclear medicine protocols are frequently updated and vary substantially across institutions, general LLM training data is inherently stale; the RAG system, indexing segmented manual content and evaluating responses with LLM-automated scoring, produced more accurate and protocol-concordant answers than base model prompting alone. This use case exemplifies a broader principle: RAG confers the largest advantage precisely where the knowledge base is specialized, dynamic, or proprietary—conditions that pervade clinical practice.

Guideline adherence is another domain where RAG has demonstrated measurable benefit. @Sarvari2025Challenges introduced two RAG-based frameworks within the Rhazes Clinician platform—generation-assisted RAG (GARAG) for evidence-based treatment planning and generation-assisted medical coding—designed to address the transparency and clinical safety concerns that have constrained LLM adoption. By anchoring responses to indexed clinical guidelines rather than relying solely on parametric knowledge, the system reduced the hallucination rate on coding tasks that require precise terminological consistency. Similarly, @Abdallah2026TrialMatchAI built TrialMatchAI, a clinical trial recruitment system grounded in a RAG framework over structured patient records and unstructured physician notes; by indexing eligibility criteria from trial registries and retrieving the most relevant criteria for each patient profile, the system achieved automated matching at a scale infeasible with manual review.

GraphRAG—an extension that represents knowledge as a structured graph rather than a flat document index—offers additional gains for reasoning tasks that require multi-hop inference across clinical entities. @Evangelista2026GraphRAGEnabled developed a proof-of-concept GraphRAG system for gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) management, combining a knowledge graph encoding clinical relationships among GDM risk factors, glycemic targets, and therapeutic interventions with an LLM reader. The knowledge graph structure enabled the system to traverse clinical reasoning chains that flat-document RAG would handle less reliably, demonstrating improved contextual relevance on GDM-specific queries. In parallel, @Lin2026Evaluating evaluated source-based LLMs that ground responses in student-uploaded learning materials via RAG algorithms for preclinical dermatology education, finding that explicit grounding in user-defined corpora reduced factual drift compared with ungrounded models and aligned with Cognitive Load Theory by using familiar materials as retrieval anchors.

RAG also offers a path toward personalized, up-to-date clinical decision support without full model retraining—a critical capability in a domain where guideline updates, newly approved drugs, and institution-specific formularies must be reflected rapidly. @Wu2025Towards proposed a foundation model framework for medication recommendation that integrates multi-source domain knowledge—including drug databases, clinical ontologies, and EHR-derived patient representations—demonstrating that multi-source retrieval substantially outperforms single-domain models on complex polypharmacy cases in the IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics.

## Domain-Adapted and Fine-Tuned Models

While prompt engineering and RAG operate on general-purpose models at inference time, fine-tuning adjusts model weights on domain-specific corpora, embedding clinical knowledge directly into the model's parameters. The resulting specialized models exhibit superior performance on in-distribution tasks but introduce trade-offs in generalizability and computational cost.

The ophthalmology-specific Language Enhanced Model for Eye (LEME), described by @Gilson2024Language, exemplifies this approach. LEME was initialized from the Llama2 70B backbone and further fine-tuned on approximately 127,000 non-copyrighted training instances drawn from ophthalmology case reports, abstracts, and open-source study materials. When benchmarked against eight general LLMs—including GPT-3.5, GPT-4, three Llama2 variants, and PMC-LLaMA—LEME demonstrated measurable gains on ophthalmology-specific question-answering, validating the principle that domain-specific fine-tuning on well-curated corpora improves task-level performance even when the base model is already large.

@Contreras2025DeLLiriuM reported DeLLiriuM, an LLM fine-tuned on structured EHR data for ICU delirium prediction. Delirium affects up to 31% of ICU patients, and early detection can enable timely intervention; DeLLiriuM's architecture adapted a foundation model to integrate multi-variate time-series EHR inputs, achieving performance superior to traditional machine-learning baselines on multi-center cohorts—a result that underscores the value of foundation model pretraining as an initialization for clinical prediction tasks.

The federated learning paradigm extends fine-tuning to settings where patient data cannot leave institutional silos. @Lal2026Ollamadriven described an Ollama-driven approach using LLMs within a federated learning framework for medical insights, enabling model updates across distributed clinical datasets without centralizing protected health information—a deployment architecture increasingly relevant as privacy regulations tighten globally.

A critical tension runs through the specialized-versus-general dichotomy. @Bedi2026Holistic's MedHELM evaluation reveals that even highly capable general models (GPT-4o, Claude 3, and others) achieve near-saturating performance on medical licensing benchmarks while exhibiting substantial gaps on practice-based tasks, suggesting that the performance ceiling for prompting general models is task-dependent. Conversely, domain-specific models fine-tuned on narrow corpora may underperform general models on tasks outside their training distribution, as noted in comparative assessments across subspecialties [@Beil2025Comparative]. The emerging consensus favors hybrid architectures—general-purpose foundations augmented by domain-specific retrieval indices and targeted fine-tuning on task-relevant data—as the optimal configuration for clinical deployment. This convergence sets the stage for the agentic systems reviewed in subsequent sections, where retrieval, reasoning, and action are integrated into autonomous pipelines capable of executing multi-step clinical research workflows.
